It results from decreased metabolism and increased lactate production due to tumor involvement. Symptoms include tachypnea, tachycardia, altered mental status, and hepatomegaly. Serum lactic acid levels can reach 10–20 mmol/L (90–180 mg/dL). Treatment focuses on the underlying disease, with severe cases requiring sodium bicarbonate or renal replacement therapy.
keyPoints: Occurs in leukemia, lymphoma, solid tumors without hypoxemia.
Symptoms: tachypnea, tachycardia, altered mental status, hepatomegaly.
Serum lactic acid levels can reach 10–20 mmol/L (90–180 mg/dL).
Causes: decreased metabolism, increased lactate production, glycolytic enzyme overexpression, mitochondrial dysfunction.
Treatment: Address underlying disease, sodium bicarbonate for severe acidosis, renal replacement therapy.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS

items.title: Tumor-Associated Hypoglycemia: Mechanisms and Management
items.content: Certain tumors, including mesenchymal tumors, hepatomas, and adrenocortical tumors, can cause hypoglycemia by secreting incompletely processed insulin-like growth factor II (IGF-II), which activates insulin receptors. This results in suppressed insulin and C-peptide levels, low growth hormone, and beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations. Management includes glucose administration or glucocorticoids if the tumor is unresectable.
keyPoints: Tumors may secrete incompletely processed IGF-II causing hypoglycemia.
Characterized by increased IGF-II to IGF-I ratio, suppressed insulin and C-peptide levels, low growth hormone, and beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations.
Management includes glucose, glucocorticoids, recombinant growth hormone, or glucagon if tumor is unresectable.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Adrenal Insufficiency in Cancer Patients
items.content: Adrenal insufficiency may be unrecognized in cancer patients due to nonspecific symptoms like nausea, vomiting, anorexia, and orthostatic hypotension. It can result from metastases, treatment with certain drugs (e.g., mitotane, ketoconazole), or rapid reduction of glucocorticoid therapy. Ipilimumab may cause autoimmune-like adrenal insufficiency, presenting 6-12 weeks post-treatment initiation.
keyPoints: Adrenal insufficiency symptoms include nausea, vomiting, anorexia, and orthostatic hypotension.
Causes: metastases, drug treatment (mitotane, ketoconazole), rapid glucocorticoid reduction.
Ipilimumab can cause autoimmune adrenal insufficiency 6-12 weeks after initiation.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.title: Acute Adrenal Insufficiency: Diagnosis and Treatment
items.content: Acute adrenal insufficiency is potentially lethal and requires immediate treatment. Diagnosis involves sampling serum cortisol and ACTH levels before initiating treatment for suspected adrenal crisis.
keyPoints: Acute adrenal insufficiency is potentially lethal.
Diagnosis involves sampling serum cortisol and ACTH levels.
Immediate treatment is crucial in suspected adrenal crisis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.title: Recognizing Tumor Lysis Syndrome (TLS) Risk Factors and Initial Evaluation
items.content: TLS is a potentially life-threatening condition that occurs due to the rapid destruction of malignant cells, leading to metabolic disturbances. It is most commonly associated with certain hematologic malignancies treated with specific therapies. Key risk factors include high tumor burden and impaired renal function. Pretreatment evaluations are crucial for identifying patients at risk.
keyPoints: TLS is characterized by hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hypocalcemia.
Most often associated with Burkitt's lymphoma, acute lymphoblastic leukemia, AML, and other rapidly proliferating lymphomas.
Increased risk in lymphoid neoplasms treated with venetoclax.
TLS usually occurs during or shortly (1–5 days) after chemotherapy.
Risk factors include high tumor burden and impaired renal function.
Pretreatment evaluations should include complete blood count, serum chemistry evaluation, and urinalysis.
High leukocyte and platelet counts may cause pseudohyperkalemia; plasma potassium should be monitored in these cases.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME

items.title: Management of Metabolic Complications in Tumor Lysis Syndrome (TLS)
items.content: Effective management of TLS involves addressing the metabolic disturbances caused by rapid tumor cell lysis. Hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, and hypocalcemia are critical components that require prompt intervention to prevent renal failure and cardiac complications.
keyPoints: Hyperuricemia can lead to uric acid nephropathy; urinary uric acid/creatinine ratio >1 indicates acute hyperuricemic nephropathy.
Hyperphosphatemia causes hypocalcemia, leading to neuromuscular irritability and tetany.
Hyperkalemia may cause ventricular arrhythmias and sudden death in renal failure.
LDH levels >1500 U/L correlate with total tumor burden and risk of TLS.
Monitor urine output closely; evaluate kidneys and retroperitoneal area by sonography/CT if baseline renal function is abnormal.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME

items.title: Prevention and Management of Tumor Lysis Syndrome (TLS)
items.content: The primary strategy for managing TLS involves risk recognition and prevention. The standard preventive approach includes the use of allopurinol and aggressive hydration to prevent uric acid crystallization in the renal tubules. Febuxostat, a nonpurine selective xanthine oxidase inhibitor, is preferred over allopurinol due to its superior serum uric acid control and fewer hypersensitivity reactions. In cases where standard prevention fails, rasburicase can be used for rapid reduction of uric acid levels.
keyPoints: Allopurinol and aggressive hydration are the standard preventive measures.
Febuxostat is preferred over allopurinol due to fewer hypersensitivity reactions and does not require dosage adjustment in mild to moderate renal impairment.
Rasburicase rapidly decreases uric acid levels but may cause hypersensitivity reactions such as bronchospasm, hypoxemia, and hypotension.
Rasburicase is contraindicated in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Tumor Lysis Syndrome

items.title: Management of Refractory Tumor Lysis Syndrome and Renal Failure
items.content: In cases where TLS is refractory to standard preventive measures, rasburicase can be effective, especially in the presence of renal failure. If TLS or oliguric/anuric renal failure occurs despite prophylaxis, early consideration for renal replacement therapy is crucial. Hemodialysis is preferred over hemofiltration due to its ability to rapidly remove cellular by-products and fluid.
keyPoints: Rasburicase can be used when standard preventive measures are insufficient, particularly in renal failure.
Renal replacement therapy should be considered early if TLS or oliguric/anuric renal failure occurs.
Hemodialysis is preferred for its rapid removal of cellular by-products and fluid.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Tumor Lysis Syndrome

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) in Antibody Infusions
items.content: Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) is a common complication following the infusion of human or humanized antibodies, characterized by symptoms such as fever, chills, nausea, asthenia, and headache. Severe manifestations like pulmonary infiltrates, ARDS, and cardiogenic shock occur rarely but require intensive care. CRS results from immune activation and cytokine release, with tocilizumab being an effective treatment for severe cases.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Fever, chills, nausea, asthenia, headache in up to 50% of patients
Severe reactions (bronchospasm, hypotension) occur in 1%
Laboratory findings: Elevated hepatic aminotransferase levels, thrombocytopenia, prolonged prothrombin time
Pathogenesis involves immune activation and cytokine release (TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-6, IL-10)
Severe CRS may require intensive support for ARDS and resistant hypotension
Tocilizumab is effective for severe or life-threatening CRS by inhibiting IL-6 signaling
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS

items.title: CAR T Cell Therapy: Managing CRS and Associated Toxicities
items.content: Chimeric Antigen Receptor (CAR) T cell therapy, while promising for cancer treatment, can lead to acute toxicity primarily in the form of CRS. This syndrome may also cause cardiac dysfunction and neurotoxicity. Management includes supportive care and tocilizumab, with a risk of hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis/macrophage activation syndrome (HLH/MAS) as part of severe CRS.
keyPoints: CAR T cell therapy can cause CRS, cardiac dysfunction, and neurotoxicity
CRS is the most common acute toxicity associated with CAR T cells
MAS may develop as a manifestation of severe CRS
Management includes supportive care and tocilizumab for CRS
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS

items.title: Mechanisms of HUS in Malignancy
items.content: Hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS) can be induced by malignancies through mechanisms such as systemic microvascular metastases, extensive bone marrow invasion, or secondary necrosis. It is associated with various cancers and certain antineoplastic drugs.
keyPoints: Malignancy-induced HUS mechanisms include systemic microvascular metastases, extensive bone marrow invasion, or secondary necrosis.
Associated malignancies: gastric, ovarian cancers, leukemias, lymphomas.
Antineoplastic drugs linked to HUS: mitomycin (most common), gemcitabine, cisplatin, bleomycin, proteasome inhibitors, VEGF inhibitors.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Laboratory Findings of HUS
items.content: HUS typically presents 4–8 weeks post-chemotherapy with symptoms like microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, renal failure, dyspnea, weakness, fatigue, oliguria, purpura. Key lab findings include severe to moderate anemia, schistocytes on smear, decreased haptoglobin, elevated LDH, and normal Coombs test.
keyPoints: Onset: 4–8 weeks post-chemotherapy; can occur months later.
Symptoms: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia (<100,000/μL), renal failure, dyspnea, weakness, fatigue, oliguria, purpura.
Lab findings: severe to moderate anemia, schistocytes on smear, reticulocytosis, decreased haptoglobin, elevated LDH, normal Coombs test.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.title: Treatment and Prognosis of Chemotherapy-Induced HUS
items.content: The treatment for chemotherapy-induced HUS is debated; options include plasmapheresis, plasma exchange, immunoadsorption, antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapies, and immunosuppression. Rituximab and eculizumab are used in specific cases, with a high case-fatality rate.
keyPoints: Treatment options: plasmapheresis, plasma exchange, immunoadsorption, antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapies, immunosuppression.
Rituximab for chemotherapy-induced HUS and ADAMTS13-deficient TTP.
Eculizumab as first-line treatment for atypical HUS; ravulizumab approved by FDA with extended half-life.
Mandatory vaccination against Neisseria meningitis before eculizumab/ravulizumab administration.
High case-fatality rate: most patients die within a few months.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.title: Pulmonary Toxicity from Cytotoxic Agents
items.content: Cytotoxic agents can cause pulmonary damage, presenting as interstitial pneumonitis, alveolitis, or pulmonary fibrosis. Symptoms include dyspnea, nonproductive cough, and tachycardia, with radiographic findings showing an interstitial pattern strongest at the lung bases.
keyPoints: Common cytotoxic agents causing pulmonary damage: bleomycin, methotrexate, busulfan, nitrosoureas, gemcitabine, mitomycin, vinorelbine, docetaxel, paclitaxel, fludarabine, pentostatin, ifosfamide.
Symptoms: dyspnea, nonproductive cough, tachycardia, bibasilar crepitant rales, end-inspiratory crackles, fever, cyanosis.
Radiographic findings: interstitial and intraalveolar patterns strongest at lung bases; hypoxemia with decreased carbon monoxide diffusing capacity.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.title: Risk Factors and Management of Gefitinib-Induced ILD
items.content: Gefitinib, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor, can cause interstitial lung disease (ILD) with high fatality rates. Risk factors include preexisting fibrosis, poor performance status, and prior thoracic irradiation. Incidence is higher in Japan (~4.5%) compared to the US (0.5%).
keyPoints: Gefitinib risk factors: preexisting fibrosis, poor performance status, prior thoracic irradiation.
Incidence of ILD with gefitinib: ~4.5% in Japan, 0.5% in the United States.
Management includes dose reduction or withdrawal and glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.title: Immunotherapy-Related Pneumonitis
items.content: Immune checkpoint inhibitors can cause immunemediated pneumonitis, a rare but life-threatening complication. Symptoms include cough, dyspnea, and fever with radiographic changes showing ground-glass patchy lesions or nodular infiltrates.
keyPoints: ICIs causing pneumonitis: nivolumab, pembrolizumab, durvalumab, avelumab, atezolizumab, cemiplimab.
Symptoms of pneumonitis: cough, dyspnea, fever; often asymptomatic radiographic changes.
Radiographic findings: ground-glass patchy lesions and/or disseminated nodular infiltrates, predominantly in lower lobes.
Treatment includes withdrawal of the drug and high-dose glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.title: Radiation Pneumonitis: Timing and Management
items.content: Radiation pneumonitis is a common side effect of thoracic radiation therapy, typically developing 2–6 months post-therapy. It presents with dyspnea, cough, low-grade fever, and hazy infiltrates on chest radiographs confined to the irradiated field. Treatment involves prednisone for symptomatic cases, tapered slowly to prevent exacerbation.
keyPoints: Radiation pneumonitis develops 2–6 months after radiotherapy completion.
Symptoms include dyspnea, cough with scanty sputum, low-grade fever, and hazy infiltrates on chest radiographs.
Prednisone is indicated for symptomatic patients; tapering should be gradual.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 2)

items.title: SBRT-Induced Pulmonary Changes
items.content: Stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) for stage I lung cancers in medically inoperable patients results in acute changes typically occurring after 3 months. The injury conforms tightly to the tumor, differing from conventional radiation therapy.
keyPoints: Acute pulmonary changes post-SBRT occur later than 3 months.
Injury shape conforms more tightly to the tumor compared to conventional radiation.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis and Imaging in Neutropenic Enterocolitis
items.content: Neutropenic enterocolitis, or typhlitis, is characterized by inflammation and necrosis of the cecum and surrounding tissues. It often presents with right lower quadrant abdominal pain, fever, neutropenia, watery diarrhea, and bacteremia. CT scans are valuable in diagnosing this condition as they may show bowel wall thickening, edema, mesenteric stranding, and ascites, which help differentiate it from other conditions like appendicitis or C. difficile colitis.
keyPoints: Neutropenic enterocolitis can involve any segment of the gastrointestinal tract.
CT scan findings: bowel wall thickening >10 mm on ultrasonogram indicates higher mortality rates.
Pneumatosis intestinalis is specific to neutropenic enterocolitis and ischemia.
Bowel wall thickness >10 mm on ultrasonogram correlates with increased mortality.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIC ENTEROCOLITIS

items.title: Management of Neutropenic Enterocolitis
items.content: The management of neutropenic enterocolitis involves rapid institution of broad-spectrum antibiotics, bowel rest, and nasogastric suction. Myeloid growth factors significantly improve outcomes. Surgical intervention is reserved for severe cases with evidence of perforation, peritonitis, gangrenous bowel, or gastrointestinal hemorrhage despite correction of coagulopathy.
keyPoints: Broad-spectrum antibiotics, bowel rest, and nasogastric suction are initial treatments.
Myeloid growth factors improve outcomes significantly.
Surgical intervention is for severe cases with perforation, peritonitis, gangrenous bowel, or hemorrhage.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIC ENTEROCOLITIS

items.subtopic: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS
items.title: Prevention and Management of Hemorrhagic Cystitis in Oncology Patients
items.content: Hemorrhagic cystitis is a significant complication associated with chemotherapy, particularly cyclophosphamide or ifosfamide. It results from the metabolite acrolein causing bladder irritation. Preventive measures include maintaining high urine flow and using mesna to detoxify harmful metabolites. If symptoms like gross hematuria and dysuria occur, supportive care and specific interventions such as bladder irrigation with alum or formalin may be employed. In severe cases, surgical options might be necessary.
keyPoints: Hemorrhagic cystitis is linked to cyclophosphamide and ifosfamide due to acrolein metabolite.
Preventive measures include high urine flow and mesna administration (20% of total dose thrice on the day of ifosfamide).
Symptoms: gross hematuria, frequency, dysuria, urgency, incontinence, nocturia.
Bladder irrigation with alum or formalin can stop bleeding; N-Acetylcysteine and prostaglandins may be effective.
Severe cases might require ligation of hypogastric arteries, urinary diversion, or cystectomy.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS

items.subtopic: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS
items.title: Identifying and Managing Hypersensitivity Reactions to Antineoplastic Drugs
items.content: Hypersensitivity reactions to antineoplastic drugs are unpredictable and can be life-threatening. These reactions often occur during or shortly after parenteral administration of agents like taxanes, platinum compounds, and biologic agents such as rituximab and trastuzumab. Skin testing may help identify patients at high risk for hypersensitivity, particularly with carboplatin exposure.
keyPoints: Hypersensitivity reactions are unpredictable and potentially life-threatening.
Commonly associated drugs: taxanes, platinum compounds, asparaginase, etoposide, procarbazine, rituximab, bevacizumab, trastuzumab, gemtuzumab, cetuximab, alemtuzumab.
Reactions often occur during or within hours of administration; taxanes may react on first or second dose.
Skin testing can identify high-risk patients for carboplatin hypersensitivity.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS

options: Lung cancer, particularly small-cell and squamous cell histologies

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically associated with superior vena cava syndrome (SVCS)?
options: Facial swelling and plethora exacerbated when supine
items.explanation: Pleural effusion occurs in only about 25% of SVCS cases, not 75%.

items.question: In patients with superior vena cava syndrome (SVCS), what is a significant risk when sedatives or general anesthesia are administered?
options: Resolution of symptoms due to collateral circulation
items.explanation: Sedatives or general anesthesia can lead to cardiac arrest or respiratory failure in patients with SVCS.

items.question: Which treatment is primarily used for Superior Vena Cava Syndrome caused by non-small-cell lung cancer?
items.explanation: Radiation therapy is the primary treatment for SVCS caused by non-small-cell lung cancer.

Used to prevent SVC stent complications

items.question: Which complication may occur after SVC stent placement?
items.explanation: Complications of SVC stent placement include hematoma at the insertion site.

options: High levels of tumor markers in pericardial fluid
items.explanation: Tumor marker measurements in pericardial fluid are not helpful for diagnosing malignant pericardial effusion.

items.question: What is the most helpful diagnostic test for evaluating pericardial disease?
items.explanation: Echocardiography is the most helpful diagnostic test for pericardial disease.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PERICARDIAL EFFUSION/TAMPONADE

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is least specific to malignant pericardial effusion?
items.explanation: Paradoxical pulse is less common in malignant pericardial disease compared to non-malignant conditions.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PERICARDIAL EFFUSION/TAMPONADE

items.question: Which treatment for acute pericardial tamponade with life-threatening hemodynamic instability is performed immediately?
items.explanation: Immediate drainage of fluid in acute pericardial tamponade is achieved by pericardiocentesis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Pericardial Effusion/Tamponade | INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION

items.question: What percentage of patients undergoing surgical drainage for malignant pericardial effusions experience recurrence after thoracoscopic pericardial fenestration?
items.explanation: About 60% of malignant pericardial effusions recur after thoracoscopic pericardial fenestration.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Pericardial Effusion/Tamponade | INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION

items.question: Which condition may paradoxically worsen hemodynamic instability following drainage of a pericardial effusion?
items.explanation: 'Postoperative low cardiac output syndrome' can worsen hemodynamic instability after drainage.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Pericardial Effusion/Tamponade | INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION

items.question: What is a common cause of intestinal obstruction in patients with advanced cancer?
items.explanation: Intestinal obstruction in advanced cancer can be caused by metastatic disease, adhesions, and drug-induced ileus.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Pericardial Effusion/Tamponade | INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION

items.question: Which medication may relieve obstructive symptoms in intestinal obstruction by inhibiting gastrointestinal secretion?
items.explanation: Octreotide relieves obstructive symptoms through its inhibitory effect on gastrointestinal secretion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Intestinal Obstruction | URINARY OBSTRUCTION

items.question: What is the most common symptom of urinary obstruction?
items.explanation: Flank pain is the most common symptom of urinary obstruction.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Intestinal Obstruction | URINARY OBSTRUCTION

items.question: What imaging modality is considered the safest and cheapest way to identify hydronephrosis?
items.explanation: Renal ultrasound is the safest and cheapest way to identify hydronephrosis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Intestinal Obstruction | URINARY OBSTRUCTION

items.subtopic: Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction
items.question: Which of the following is an indication for immediate palliative urinary diversion in cases of urinary obstruction?
options: Flank pain, sepsis, or fistula formation
items.explanation: Immediate palliative urinary diversion is indicated for obstruction associated with flank pain, sepsis, or fistula formation.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction

items.subtopic: Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction
items.question: Which intervention is associated with a significant rate of pyelonephritis in urinary obstruction management?
items.explanation: The placement of a nephrostomy is associated with a significant rate of pyelonephritis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction

items.subtopic: Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction
items.question: In cases of bladder outlet obstruction due to malignancy, which procedure can be used for urinary drainage?
items.explanation: A suprapubic cystostomy can be used for urinary drainage in cases of bladder outlet obstruction due to malignancy.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction

items.question: What is the most common initial symptom in patients with spinal cord compression?
options: Loss of bowel or bladder control
items.explanation: The most common initial symptom is localized back pain and tenderness due to vertebrae involvement by a tumor.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 1)

items.question: Which cancer is the most frequent cause of malignant spinal cord compression?
items.explanation: Lung cancer is the most common cause of malignant spinal cord compression.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 1)

items.question: Which part of the spine is most commonly affected by metastatic tumors causing spinal cord compression?
items.explanation: The thoracic spine is the most common site affected by metastatic tumors causing spinal cord compression.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 1)

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of radicular pain from thoracic roots?
options: Unilateral and radiates down the arm
Bilateral and described as tight, band-like constriction around the thorax and abdomen
items.explanation: Radicular pain from thoracic roots is often bilateral and described as a feeling of tight, band-like constriction around the thorax and abdomen.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging procedure is considered the best choice for providing a full-length image of the spinal cord in cases of suspected cord compression?
items.explanation: MRI provides a full-length image of the cord and is the imaging procedure of choice for detecting spinal cord compression.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 2)

items.question: What does the 'winking owl' sign indicate in plain films?
items.explanation: The 'winking owl' sign indicates erosion of the pedicles, which is an early radiologic finding of vertebral tumor.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients with cord compression have multiple epidural metastases?
items.explanation: Multiple epidural metastases are noted in 25% of patients with cord compression.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 2)

items.question: What is generally the initial treatment of choice for most patients with spinal cord compression (SCC)?
items.explanation: Radiation therapy plus glucocorticoids is generally the initial treatment of choice for most patients with SCC.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Spinal Cord Compression

items.question: In the management of spinal cord compression, what does the NOMS acronym stand for?
items.explanation: NOMS stands for Neurologic, Oncologic, Mechanical, and Systemic factors.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Spinal Cord Compression

items.question: What is necessary in patients with high-grade SCC due to radioresistant tumors?
items.explanation: In patients with high-grade SCC due to radioresistant tumors, separation surgery followed by SBRT is necessary.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Spinal Cord Compression

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure
items.question: Which of the following cancers most commonly metastasize to the brain?
items.explanation: Lung and breast cancers, along with melanoma, are the most common cancers that metastasize to the brain.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure
items.question: What is the initial treatment for symptomatic patients with brain metastases?
items.explanation: Dexamethasone is the best initial treatment for all symptomatic patients with brain metastases.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure
items.question: Which imaging modality is superior to CT scan in detecting meningeal involvement and small lesions in the brain?
items.explanation: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI is more sensitive than CT at revealing meningeal involvement and small lesions.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure

items.question: Which type of cancer is most commonly associated with leptomeningeal seeding after resection or stereotactic radiotherapy for brain metastases?
items.explanation: Melanoma, breast, and lung cancers are frequently associated with leptomeningeal seeding post-treatment.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEOPLASTIC MENINGITIS

items.question: What is a common diagnostic challenge when identifying neoplastic meningitis through CSF cytology?
items.explanation: Up to 40% of patients may have false-negative CSF cytology, complicating diagnosis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEOPLASTIC MENINGITIS

items.question: Which MRI finding is suggestive of neoplastic meningitis?
items.explanation: MRI findings such as leptomeningeal, subependymal, dural, or cranial nerve enhancement suggest neoplastic meningitis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEOPLASTIC MENINGITIS

items.question: Which intrathecal chemotherapy agents are commonly used to treat leptomeningeal disease?
items.explanation: Intrathecal chemotherapy for leptomeningeal disease commonly includes methotrexate, cytarabine, or thiotepa.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Neoplastic Meningitis | SEIZURES

items.question: Which type of cancer is most responsive to therapy in cases of neoplastic meningitis?
items.explanation: Among solid tumors, breast cancer responds best to therapy for neoplastic meningitis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Neoplastic Meningitis | SEIZURES

items.question: What is the most common cause of seizures in patients with cancer?
items.explanation: Metastatic disease to the CNS is the most common cause of seizures in patients with cancer.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Neoplastic Meningitis | SEIZURES

items.question: Which anticonvulsant medication is preferred for patients with CNS metastases due to its lack of interaction with the hepatic CYP450 system?
items.explanation: Levetiracetam is preferred because it does not alter the metabolism of antitumor agents and is not metabolized by the hepatic CYP450 system.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Seizures | PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS

items.question: Which condition is associated with hyperleukocytosis in acute myeloid leukemia and presents with stupor, headache, and visual disturbances?
items.explanation: Leukostasis syndrome is associated with hyperleukocytosis and presents with symptoms like stupor, headache, and visual disturbances.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Seizures | PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS

items.question: What is the recommended initial treatment for patients presenting with hyperleukocytosis while awaiting diagnostic confirmation?
items.explanation: Hydroxyurea is used to rapidly reduce a high blast cell count while the diagnostic workup is in progress.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Seizures | PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS

items.question: Which of the following is considered a first-line definitive procedure for managing massive hemoptysis?
items.explanation: Bronchial artery embolization is considered a first-line definitive procedure for managing hemoptysis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOPTYSIS

items.question: What percentage of patients with lung cancer experience hemoptysis at some point during their illness?
items.explanation: Up to 20% of patients with lung cancer have hemoptysis some time in their course.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOPTYSIS

items.question: What is the typical duration and resolution method for postembolization syndrome?
items.explanation: Postembolization syndrome lasts 5–7 days and resolves with symptomatic treatment.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOPTYSIS

items.subtopic: AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS
items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of malignant upper airway obstruction?
options: Invasion from an adjacent primary tumor, most commonly lung cancer.
Primary tumors in the gastrointestinal tract without metastasis.
Benign growths such as vocal cord nodules.
Extrinsic compression by non-malignant mediastinal masses.
items.explanation: Invasion from an adjacent primary tumor, most commonly lung cancer, is the most common cause of malignant upper airway obstruction.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS

items.subtopic: AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS
items.question: Which treatment can provide temporary relief for airway obstruction?
items.explanation: Cool, humidified oxygen and glucocorticoids can provide temporary relief for airway obstruction.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS

items.subtopic: AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS
items.question: What is a potential treatment for severe lactic acidosis?
options: Sodium bicarbonate if acidosis is very severe.
items.explanation: Sodium bicarbonate should be added if lactic acidosis is very severe or if hydrogen ion production is rapid and uncontrolled.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS

items.question: Which of the following tumors are associated with hypoglycemia due to secretion of incompletely processed insulin-like growth factor II (IGF-II)?
items.explanation: Mesenchymal tumors can cause hypoglycemia by secreting incompletely processed IGF-II.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.question: What is a potential cause of artifactual hypoglycemia?
items.explanation: Artifactual hypoglycemia can be caused by hyperleukocytosis, which increases glucose consumption in the test tube.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.question: Which medication used to manage cancer and HIV-related cachexia may suppress plasma levels of cortisol and ACTH?
items.explanation: Megestrol acetate can suppress plasma levels of cortisol and ACTH, potentially leading to adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.question: Which condition may present with headache, visual field defects, and pituitary hormone deficiencies?
items.explanation: Autoimmune hypophysitis can present with headache, visual field defects, and pituitary hormone deficiencies.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.question: Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with tumor lysis syndrome (TLS)?
items.explanation: TLS is most often associated with the treatment of Burkitt's lymphoma, acute lymphoblastic leukemia, AML, and other rapidly proliferating lymphomas.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME

items.question: Which laboratory finding is a strong indicator of uric acid nephropathy in TLS?
options: Uric acid crystals in the urine
items.explanation: The finding of uric acid crystals in the urine is strong evidence for uric acid nephropathy.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME

items.question: What factor can lead to pseudohyperkalemia in patients at risk for TLS?
options: High serum levels of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)
Lysis of leukocytes and platelets after blood is drawn
Decreased renal blood flow due to vasoconstriction
Deposition of calcium phosphate in the kidney
items.explanation: High leukocyte and platelet counts may artificially elevate potassium levels ('pseudohyperkalemia') due to lysis of these cells after the blood is drawn.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME

items.question: Which medication is contraindicated in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency due to its mechanism of action?
items.explanation: Rasburicase is contraindicated in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency due to the risk of hemolysis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Tumor Lysis Syndrome

items.question: Which agent is preferred for rapid reduction of uric acid levels when standard preventive measures are insufficient?
items.explanation: Rasburicase acts rapidly to decrease uric acid levels, making it effective when standard measures are insufficient.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Tumor Lysis Syndrome

items.question: What is the recommended renal replacement therapy for managing TLS complicated by renal failure?
items.explanation: Hemodialysis is preferred for managing TLS complicated by renal failure due to its efficiency in removing cellular by-products and fluid.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Tumor Lysis Syndrome

items.question: Which of the following symptoms are commonly associated with the initial infusion of human or humanized antibodies?
options: Fever, chills, nausea, asthenia, and headache
items.explanation: These symptoms occur in up to half of treated patients during the initial infusion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS

items.question: What is a rare but severe manifestation that can occur with human antibody infusions?
items.explanation: Severe manifestations like ARDS occur rarely with human antibody infusions.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS

items.question: Which medication is considered effective for treating severe or life-threatening cytokine release syndrome (CRS)?
items.explanation: Tocilizumab is effective for severe or life-threatening CRS by inhibiting IL-6 signaling.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS

items.question: Which antineoplastic drugs have been associated with the development of hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS)?
options: Mitomycin, gemcitabine, cisplatin, bleomycin, and proteasome inhibitors
items.explanation: Mitomycin, gemcitabine, cisplatin, bleomycin, and proteasome inhibitors are associated with HUS.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.question: What is the typical time frame for the onset of HUS after chemotherapy?
items.explanation: HUS usually has its onset 4–8 weeks after the last dose of chemotherapy.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.question: Which laboratory finding is NOT characteristic of HUS?
options: Severe to moderate anemia with red blood cell fragmentation
items.explanation: The serum bilirubin level is usually normal or slightly elevated in HUS.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.question: What treatment has been considered first-line for atypical HUS?
items.explanation: Eculizumab, a complement inhibitor, is considered first-line treatment for atypical HUS.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.question: Which cytotoxic agent is associated with noncardiogenic pulmonary edema?
items.explanation: Cytosine arabinoside has been associated with noncardiogenic pulmonary edema.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.question: Which molecular targeted agent is known to cause interstitial lung disease (ILD) with a high fatality rate?
items.explanation: Gefitinib is associated with ILD that has a high fatality rate.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.question: Which immune checkpoint inhibitor can enhance antitumor activity by blocking negative regulators of T-cell function?
items.explanation: All listed ICIs block negative regulators of T-cell function.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.question: What is a common presentation in patients with pulmonary toxicity due to cytotoxic agents?
options: Interstitial and intraalveolar pattern on chest radiograph
items.explanation: Interstitial pneumonitis is a frequent presentation in patients with pulmonary toxicity due to cytotoxic agents.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.question: Which factor does NOT increase the risk of radiation pneumonitis?
options: Use of concurrent chemoradiation with paclitaxel
items.explanation: A smaller irradiated lung volume decreases the risk for radiation pneumonitis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 2)

items.question: What is a common clinical feature of acute radiation pneumonitis?
items.explanation: Radiation pneumonitis typically presents with dyspnea, cough with scanty sputum, low-grade fever, and an initial hazy infiltrate.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 2)

items.question: When should prednisone be administered to patients after radiation therapy?
options: For fever or other symptoms of pneumonitis
Only if a lung biopsy confirms pneumonitis
items.explanation: Prednisone should be administered to patients with fever or other symptoms of radiation pneumonitis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a more specific finding for neutropenic enterocolitis?
options: Bowel wall thickness >10 mm on ultrasonogram
Marked bowel wall thickening in the cecum
items.explanation: Pneumatosis intestinalis is a more specific finding for neutropenic enterocolitis and ischemia.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIC ENTEROCOLITIS

items.question: What imaging modality may help differentiate neutropenic colitis from other abdominal disorders?
items.explanation: A CT scan can show bowel wall thickening and help differentiate neutropenic colitis from other conditions.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIC ENTEROCOLITIS

items.question: Which treatment is reserved for severe cases of neutropenic enterocolitis with complications?
items.explanation: Surgical intervention is reserved for severe cases with complications like perforation or peritonitis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIC ENTEROCOLITIS

items.subtopic: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS
items.question: Which agent is used to detoxify metabolites of ifosfamide and cyclophosphamide in the prevention of hemorrhagic cystitis?
items.explanation: Mesna is coadministered with ifosfamide and cyclophosphamide to detoxify their metabolites.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS

items.subtopic: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS
items.question: What is a common cause of late-onset hemorrhagic cystitis in the bone marrow transplantation setting?
items.explanation: Late-onset hemorrhagic cystitis in the BMT setting is usually due to polyoma virus BKV.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS

items.subtopic: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS
items.question: Which treatment has been used successfully for both BKV-associated and cyclophosphamide-induced hemorrhagic cystitis during hematopoietic stem cell transplantation?
items.explanation: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy has been used successfully for BKV-associated and cyclophosphamide-induced hemorrhagic cystitis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS

items.subtopic: Oncologic Emergencies | SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME
items.statement: The diagnosis of superior vena cava syndrome (SVCS) is primarily based on imaging findings.
items.explanation: The diagnosis of SVCS is clinical, although imaging can support the diagnosis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Oncologic Emergencies | SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME

items.subtopic: Oncologic Emergencies | SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME
items.statement: In young adults with a mediastinal mass and superior vena cava syndrome (SVCS), lymphoma should be considered in the differential diagnosis.
items.explanation: Lymphoma is a leading cause of SVCS in young adults presenting with a mediastinal mass.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Oncologic Emergencies | SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME

items.subtopic: Superior Vena Cava Syndrome | SVCS AND CENTRAL VENOUS CATHETERS IN ADULTS
items.statement: SVCS mortality is primarily related to caval obstruction.
items.explanation: The mortality associated with SVCS relates to the underlying cause, not the caval obstruction.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Superior Vena Cava Syndrome | SVCS AND CENTRAL VENOUS CATHETERS IN ADULTS

items.subtopic: Superior Vena Cava Syndrome | SVCS AND CENTRAL VENOUS CATHETERS IN ADULTS
items.statement: Fibrinolytic therapy can be used for early-detected SVCS related to central venous catheters.
items.explanation: SVCS detected early in the setting of central venous catheters can be treated with fibrinolytic therapy.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Superior Vena Cava Syndrome | SVCS AND CENTRAL VENOUS CATHETERS IN ADULTS

items.statement: Pericardial fluid cytology is diagnostic in most patients with malignant pericardial effusion.
items.explanation: Cytologic examination of pericardial fluid is diagnostic in most patients.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PERICARDIAL EFFUSION/TAMPONADE

items.statement: Most patients with pericardial metastasis are symptomatic.
items.explanation: Most patients with pericardial metastasis are asymptomatic.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PERICARDIAL EFFUSION/TAMPONADE

items.statement: Thoracoscopic pericardial fenestration is most effective for malignant causes of pericardial effusions.
items.explanation: Thoracoscopic pericardial fenestration has a high recurrence rate (60%) in malignant cases.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Pericardial Effusion/Tamponade | INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION

items.statement: Intestinal obstruction due to adhesions from previous operations is a benign cause.
items.explanation: Adhesions are a common benign cause of intestinal obstruction.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Pericardial Effusion/Tamponade | INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION

items.statement: Laparoscopy can be used both for diagnosis and treatment of malignant bowel obstruction.
items.explanation: Laparoscopy can diagnose and treat malignant bowel obstruction in some cases.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Intestinal Obstruction | URINARY OBSTRUCTION

items.statement: Radiation therapy to pelvic tumors cannot cause ureteral obstruction.
items.explanation: Radiation therapy to pelvic tumors may cause fibrosis and subsequent ureteral obstruction.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Intestinal Obstruction | URINARY OBSTRUCTION

items.subtopic: Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction
items.statement: Pain and secondary infection are common symptoms of malignant biliary obstruction.
items.explanation: Pain and secondary infection are uncommon in malignant biliary obstruction.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction

items.subtopic: Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction
items.statement: Endoscopic ultrasonography-guided biliary drainage is effective, especially when standard ERCP fails.
items.explanation: Endoscopic ultrasonography-guided biliary drainage is a safe and effective method of biliary drainage in patients with malignant biliary obstruction, particularly when standard ERCP failed.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction

items.statement: Metastatic tumors more frequently affect the cervical spine than any other part of the bony skeleton.
items.explanation: The thoracic spine is most commonly affected by metastatic tumors, not the cervical spine.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 1)

items.statement: Patients with spinal cord compression often present with loss of bowel or bladder control as an initial symptom.
items.explanation: Loss of bowel or bladder control usually occurs late in the course, not as an initial symptom.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 1)

items.statement: A normal appearance on plain films of the spine excludes the diagnosis of cancer.
items.explanation: A normal appearance on plain films does not exclude the diagnosis of cancer.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 2)

items.statement: Bone scans are more specific than spinal radiography for detecting cord compression.
items.explanation: Bone scans are sensitive but less specific than spinal radiography for detecting cord compression.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 2)

items.statement: Percutaneous vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty can be used for stabilization of painful pathologic compression fractures without spinal instability.
items.explanation: Patients with painful pathologic compression fractures without spinal instability may benefit from percutaneous vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Spinal Cord Compression

items.statement: The histology of the tumor is not an important determinant of recovery and survival in SCC.
items.explanation: The histology of the tumor is an important determinant of both recovery and survival.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Spinal Cord Compression

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure
items.statement: Hydrocephalus can result from obstruction of CSF circulation due to a tumor mass.
items.explanation: The tumor mass and surrounding edema may cause obstruction of the circulation of CSF, leading to hydrocephalus.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure
items.statement: Whole-brain radiation therapy is always preferred over SRS for patients with multiple brain metastases.
items.explanation: The treatment of a larger number of intracranial metastases remains controversial, and more patients now receive SRS due to cognitive dysfunction associated with whole-brain radiation.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure

items.statement: Radiolabeled CSF flow studies are abnormal in all patients with neoplastic meningitis.
items.explanation: Abnormalities occur in up to 70% of patients, not all.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEOPLASTIC MENINGITIS

items.statement: Placement of a ventriculoperitoneal shunt may palliate symptoms in patients with intracranial hypertension due to neoplastic meningitis.
items.explanation: A ventriculoperitoneal shunt can effectively alleviate symptoms related to hydrocephalus.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEOPLASTIC MENINGITIS

items.statement: Seizures are more frequent in metastatic brain lesions than in primary brain tumors.
items.explanation: Seizures occur more frequently in primary brain tumors than in metastatic brain lesions.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Neoplastic Meningitis | SEIZURES

items.statement: RPLS is characterized by symmetric posterior cerebral white matter vasogenic edema on CT/MRI.
items.explanation: Reversible posterior leukoencephalopathy syndrome (RPLS) includes symmetric posterior cerebral white matter vasogenic edema.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Neoplastic Meningitis | SEIZURES

items.statement: Prophylactic anticonvulsant therapy is recommended for all patients with CNS metastases.
items.explanation: Prophylactic anticonvulsant therapy is not recommended; treatment should be initiated only if seizures have been demonstrated.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Seizures | PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS

items.statement: Pulmonary leukostasis may present with respiratory distress and hypoxemia.
items.explanation: Pulmonary leukostasis can indeed present as respiratory distress and hypoxemia, progressing to respiratory failure.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Seizures | PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS

items.statement: Massive hemoptysis is defined as more than 600 mL of blood produced in 24 hours.
items.explanation: Massive hemoptysis is defined as >200–600 mL of blood produced in 24 h.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOPTYSIS

items.statement: Recurrent hemoptysis usually responds to a second embolization procedure.
items.explanation: Recurrent hemoptysis usually responds to a second embolization procedure.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOPTYSIS

items.subtopic: AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS
items.statement: SIADH is the most common cause of hyponatremia in cancer patients.
items.explanation: SIADH is indeed the most common cause of hyponatremia among patients with cancer.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS

items.subtopic: AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS
items.statement: Lactic acidosis is a common preterminal event in many malignancies.
items.explanation: Lactic acidosis associated with sepsis and circulatory failure is indeed a common preterminal event in many malignancies.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS

items.statement: Primary adrenal insufficiency may develop due to metastases replacing both adrenal glands.
items.explanation: Metastases from cancers such as lung, breast, colon, or kidney can replace both adrenal glands, leading to primary adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.statement: Autoimmune adrenalitis is more frequent with CTLA-4 inhibitors than with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors.
items.explanation: Autoimmune adrenalitis occurs more frequently with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors compared to CTLA-4 inhibitors.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.statement: Hyperphosphatemia in TLS can lead to severe neuromuscular irritability and tetany.
items.explanation: Hyperphosphatemia produces a reciprocal depression in serum calcium, causing severe neuromuscular irritability and tetany.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME

items.statement: TLS is rarely associated with solid tumors.
items.explanation: TLS may be seen with chronic leukemias and, rarely, with solid tumors.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME

items.statement: Urinary alkalization with sodium bicarbonate is recommended as part of the standard preventive approach for tumor lysis syndrome.
items.explanation: Urinary alkalization with sodium bicarbonate is no longer recommended due to increased risk of intratubular crystallization.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Tumor Lysis Syndrome

items.statement: Febuxostat requires dosage adjustment in patients with mild to moderate renal impairment.
items.explanation: Febuxostat does not require dosage adjustment in patients with mild to moderate renal impairment.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Tumor Lysis Syndrome

items.statement: Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) can lead to hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH)/macrophage activation syndrome (MAS).
items.explanation: HLH/MAS is a manifestation of severe CRS.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS

items.statement: Cardiogenic shock occurs in 1% of patients receiving human or humanized antibody infusions.
items.explanation: Severe manifestations like cardiogenic shock occur rarely, not as frequently as 1%.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS

items.statement: The Coombs test is positive in patients with HUS.
items.explanation: The Coombs test is negative in HUS.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.statement: Ravulizumab has an extended half-life compared to eculizumab.
items.explanation: Ravulizumab is engineered from eculizumab and has an extended half-life.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.statement: Patients treated with ICIs may experience immunemediated pneumonitis as a life-threatening complication.
items.explanation: Immunemediated pneumonitis is rare but can be life-threatening in patients treated with ICIs.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.statement: Supplemental oxygen should always be given at the highest possible F10 to manage pulmonary toxicity.
items.explanation: Patients should be managed with the lowest F10 sufficient to maintain hemoglobin saturation.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.statement: Radiation-induced lung toxicity is independent of the irradiated lung volume.
items.explanation: The risk for radiation pneumonitis increases with larger irradiated lung fields.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 2)

items.statement: Most acute changes after SBRT occur within the first 3 months.
items.explanation: Acute changes after SBRT typically occur later than 3 months post-treatment.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 2)

items.statement: Neutropenic enterocolitis can involve any segment of the gastrointestinal tract.
items.explanation: It may complicate treatment in various segments including small intestine, appendix, and colon.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIC ENTEROCOLITIS

items.statement: Plain abdominal films are highly valuable for diagnosing neutropenic enterocolitis.
items.explanation: Plain abdominal films are generally of little value in the diagnosis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIC ENTEROCOLITIS

items.subtopic: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS
items.statement: Hemorrhagic cystitis can be caused by radiation therapy.
items.explanation: Radiation therapy is listed as a cause of hemorrhagic cystitis.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS

items.subtopic: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS
items.statement: Antiviral therapy for viral hemorrhagic cystitis has been widely approved and is commonly used.
items.explanation: No antiviral therapy is approved, although cidofovir was reported to be effective in a small series.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS | HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS TO ANTINEOPLASTIC DRUGS

items.subtopic: Oncologic Emergencies | SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME
items.assertion: Patients with superior vena cava syndrome (SVCS) often present with neck and facial swelling.
items.reason: The obstruction of the SVC leads to reduced venous return from the head, neck, and upper extremities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Oncologic Emergencies | SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME

items.subtopic: Oncologic Emergencies | SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME
items.assertion: A normal chest radiograph excludes the diagnosis of superior vena cava syndrome (SVCS).
items.reason: Characteristic findings other than widened mediastinum can still be present in SVCS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; a normal chest radiograph does not exclude SVCS if other characteristic findings are present.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Oncologic Emergencies | SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME

items.assertion: Intestinal pseudoobstruction can be caused by paraneoplastic neuropathy.
items.reason: Paraneoplastic neuropathy involves IgG antibodies reactive to neurons of the myenteric and submucosal plexuses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Pericardial Effusion/Tamponade | INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION

items.assertion: CT enterocysis is used to diagnose high-grade small-bowel obstruction.
items.reason: CT enterocysis involves infusion of water-soluble contrast through a nasoenteric tube followed by CT imaging.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; CT enterocysis helps in diagnosing low-grade small-bowel obstruction.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Pericardial Effusion/Tamponade | INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION

items.subtopic: Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction
items.assertion: Palliative intervention is indicated only in patients with disabling pruritus resistant to medical treatment, severe malabsorption, or infection.
items.reason: Stenting under radiographic control, surgical bypass, or radiation therapy may alleviate the obstruction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Urinary Obstruction | MALIGNANT BILIARY OBSTRUCTION | Malignant Biliary Obstruction

items.assertion: Cauda equina syndrome is characterized by low back pain and diminished sensation over the buttocks.
items.reason: This reflects compression of nerve roots as they form the cauda equina after leaving the spinal cord.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with progressive neurologic symptoms should have frequent neurologic examinations.
items.reason: This is because spinal cord compression can be reversed if treated early.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not correctly explain why frequent examinations are necessary.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 1)

items.assertion: MRI is as good as or better than myelography plus postmyelogram CT scan in detecting metastatic epidural disease with cord compression.
items.reason: MRI can show the replacement of bone marrow by tumor and visualize intraspinal extradural masses compressing the cord.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why MRI is preferred.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure
items.assertion: Patients with increased intracranial pressure may require shunt placement.
items.reason: Some patients benefit from shunt placement if they have hydrocephalus associated with increased intracranial pressure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | Increased Intracranial Pressure

items.assertion: Patients with neoplastic meningitis from acute leukemia or lymphoma may be cured of their CNS disease.
items.reason: This is possible if the systemic disease can be eliminated.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Neoplastic Meningitis | SEIZURES

items.assertion: Leukostasis is a common feature in patients with chronic myeloid leukemia.
items.reason: Leukostasis rarely occurs in the high white cell counts associated with chronic myeloid leukemia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason correctly states that leukostasis is rare in chronic myeloid leukemia.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · Seizures | PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS

items.subtopic: AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS
items.assertion: Lactic acidosis in cancer patients may be related to tumor cell overexpression of certain glycolytic enzymes.
items.reason: Mitochondrial dysfunction can contribute to increased lactate production.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but mitochondrial dysfunction is not the direct explanation for the overexpression of glycolytic enzymes.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION | HYPERCALCEMIA | SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE SECRETION OF ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE | LACTIC ACIDOSIS

items.assertion: Ipilimumab can cause autoimmune-like enterocolitis.
items.reason: Ipilimumab is an anti-CTLA-4 antibody used in cancer treatment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.assertion: High-dose glucocorticoids improve pituitary function recovery in hypophysitis.
items.reason: Glucocorticoid treatment is used for autoimmune-like enterocolitis caused by ipilimumab.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; high-dose glucocorticoids may not improve pituitary function recovery, while the reason is true but unrelated.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HYPOGLYCEMIA | ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.assertion: The pathogenesis of cancer treatment-related HUS involves endothelial damage.
items.reason: Primary forms of HUS/TTP are related to a decrease in processing of von Willebrand factor by ADAMTS13.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not related to cancer treatment-related HUS.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.assertion: Vaccination against Neisseria meningitis is mandatory before administering eculizumab.
items.reason: Eculizumab increases the risk of meningococcal infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.assertion: Gefitinib-associated ILD has a higher incidence in Japan compared to the United States.
items.reason: Preexisting fibrosis, poor performance status, and prior thoracic irradiation are independent risk factors for gefitinib-induced ILD.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the higher incidence in Japan.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)

items.assertion: Glucocorticoids can provide symptomatic relief for pulmonary toxicity related to chemotherapy.
items.reason: Specific chemotherapy should always be started promptly in cases of leukemic infiltration.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason relates to leukemic infiltration and not glucocorticoid use.
items.reference: Oncologic Emergencies · NEUTROPENIA AND INFECTION | PULMONARY INFILTRATES (part 1)
